_stage: CPAL Stage 1 — Wrong-Model Identification + LP Level Classification

_concept_meta.java_concept: Type system, concatenation. Error: cannot convert int to String (or vice versa).

_input_recap.student_message: I'm trying to print a message with someone's age but Java keeps complaining. In Python this just works — I thought + would just make them into a string. Why is this so complicated?
_input_recap.compiler_error:
Greeting.java:6: error: bad operand types for binary operator '+'
        int total = age + 5 + name;
                            ^
  first type:  int
  second type: String
diagnostic_phrases_extracted: I thought + would just make them into a string

wrong_model_identification.wrong_model_description: The + operator always concatenates. int + String should produce a string automatically the way Python does.
wrong_model_identification.wrong_model_origin: Python: str(x) is implicit in many contexts
wrong_model_identification.matched_signal: I thought + would just make them into a string
wrong_model_identification.match_evidence: Near-verbatim match. Priya literally cites Python in her message ('In Python this just works'), which is the documented origin of TM-B. She also reproduces the second documented signal almost word-for-word ('I thought + would just make them into a string').

alternatives_considered.wrong_belief_short: Type is a label for humans, not a constraint Java enforces
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: TM-A students don't reference + at all — they don't understand types are checked. Priya understands types are different but expects + to bridge them.

alternatives_considered.wrong_belief_short: Java will auto-convert types when needed (calculator metaphor)
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: TM-C is closer than TM-A but lacks the Python-specific framing. Priya's appeal to Python ('this just works') is the TM-B signature.

lp_signals_detected.signal: Reports the compile error but cannot explain the type constraint
lp_signals_detected.evidence: 'Java keeps complaining' + 'why is this so complicated' — registers the error as a complaint, not a rule
lp_signals_detected.rubric_match: L1: 'Sees the compile error but cannot say why'

lp_signals_detected.signal: Treats + as universally polymorphic across types
lp_signals_detected.evidence: Wrong model TM-B held → has not yet reached L2's 'int and String are different types and + means something different for each'

expert_benchmark_key_ideas: Java checks operand types at compile time, before any computation happens.
+ is overloaded: String + anything → String concatenation (the right-hand side is converted to String). int + int → integer addition.
int + String is unambiguous if String is on either side (becomes concatenation), but int = String + int cannot fit into an int variable — that's a different mistake to be aware of.
Python's behaviour comes from runtime type coercion in __add__; Java decides at compile time and refuses ambiguity.

downstream_effects.stage_2_intervention_filter: Restrict to L1→L2 interventions: side_by_side_python_vs_java, type_signature_walkthrough. Exclude trace_scaffold (L2 plateau intervention) and transfer_task (L3+).

stage_3_prompt_sections.LP-1_current_understanding: Student treats + as polymorphic across all types because Python does
stage_3_prompt_sections.LP-2_wrong_model_context: TM-B (Python carry-over). LLM is told to bridge from Python explicitly, not to dismiss it.
stage_3_prompt_sections.LP-3_target_understanding: By end of session: student can say 'String + int becomes a String because + is concatenation when one side is String. int + String at the start of int total = ... fails because the result is a String and int cannot hold it.'
